ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mutton Mysore; mapped to the closest parent class 'mutton dish' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat dish — mutton/goat, cooked, with sauce or gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mutton Mysore; A0CKP (cooked meat dish with sauce) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Mutton Mysore or equivalent South Indian mutton curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Mysore as a side dish at a Singapore Indian restaurant or hawker stall is approximately 150 g, representing one ladle of curry with 3–4 pieces of mutton.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Mysore — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Mysore (cr_id G00010682), which is a canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this source. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at approximately 4.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked goat meat in curry preparation (USDA SR Legacy #17168 and comparable curry dish profiles), documented as an approximation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat Curry Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database; Lamb/Mutton Curry Reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Mysore in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous profile of South Asian meat curry dishes with onion-tomato gravy and minimal starch content. The carbohydrate content is 7.44 g/100g, predominantly from onion, tomato, and cashew. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 1.50) / 100 = 3.9; rounded to 2.6 per 100g equivalent. GL per serving (150g) = (35 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.9. The low GI is consistent with high protein (14.05 g) and moderate fat (11.58 g) content which slow gastric emptying. Estimation basis: University of Sydney GI Database category anchors for meat-based curry dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g — borderline-to-high for a single dish component
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mutton Mysore to a small portion (≤100 g) and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content of 467 mg/100g can contribute substantially to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; pairing with low-sodium vegetables and avoiding additional salty condiments is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Mysore is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (≤150 g), as the low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise; avoid pairing with large servings of white rice or roti to keep the overall meal glycaemic load low.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g estimated) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from red meat and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Mysore in moderation (≤100 g per occasion), as the estimated saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from red meat contribute to LDL burden; the high sodium (467 mg/100g) further elevates cardiovascular risk, and substituting with leaner cuts or reducing portion frequency is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid, omega-6) and nightshade ingredients (tomato, dried chilli) amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Mysore, as mutton is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory pathways, the estimated saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold, and nightshade ingredients (tomato, dried red chilli) may trigger joint flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Mysore is a South Indian braised mutton curry originating from the Mysore region of Karnataka, India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian hawker stalls and restaurants. The dish is prepared by marinating bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in a blend of aromatic spices, then slow-cooking them in a rich onion-tomato gravy tempered with curry leaves, mustard seeds, star anise, and cashew nuts. The result is a deeply flavoured, moderately thick curry with tender meat that carries warm, earthy spice notes from the whole spices and a mild heat from dried red chillies. Per 100 g, Mutton Mysore provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and only 7.44 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100g, which is a concern for hypertension management. The dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and high protein profile, but should be consumed in controlled portions by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the red meat content and nightshade ingredients. Mutton Mysore is typically served as a side dish with rice, roti, or parotta at lunch or dinner, and is a staple of South Indian and Singapore Indian cuisine.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Mutton Mysore (cr_id G00010682) with HPB-assigned confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. Saturated fat is the only value estimated from an analogous USDA source, which reduces overall confidence marginally but not below high given the strong primary source for all other fields.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is an approximation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked goat meat in curry preparation, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for Mutton Mysore are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database category anchors for South Asian meat curry dishes with onion-tomato gravy; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Mysore exists in the database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving ≈ 3.9 (low). 4. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate anchors; no exact ontology entry exists for Mutton Mysore. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the dish's Indian Muslim hawker context in Singapore; individual preparation should be verified. 6. The description references coconut milk in the input health_suitability notes, but the canonical HPB description does not list coconut milk as an ingredient; the dish is described as onion-tomato based. Coconut milk cautions from the input have been retained in health context where relevant but the primary ingredient list follows the HPB canonical description.
